coredata.dc:title: Corrosion protection and antibacterial performance of a chitosan/salicylate coating electrogenerated on a magnesium alloy

Saidman, S.B.

coredata.dc:description: In this work, chitosan and chitosan/salicylate coatings were prepared by EPD on AZ91D Mg alloy to improve the corrosion resistance of the substrate in a simulated physiological solution. The effect of two concentrations of salicylate on the morphology and anticorrosive behaviour of the coatings was evaluated. The results showed that the addition of 0.50 g/L sodium salicylate to the chitosan formulation decreased the corrosion rate of the chitosan coating from 0.0025 ± 0.0003 to 0.0007 ± 0.0004 mA cm−2. From the results of electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) and Fourier Transform Infrared (FTIR) analysis, it can be concluded that the presence of salicylate in the coating leads to a self-healing effect that contributes to an improved corrosion protection. In addition, the presence of salicylate in the coating has been shown to increase antibacterial activity against both Gram-positive and Gram-negative bacteria. It has been demonstrated that salicylate can be released from the coating, which has an anti-inflammatory effect. In this way, a novel chitosan/salicylate coating was developed for the first time on a Mg alloy for bactericidal effect, release of salicylate and corrosion protection in simulated physiological solution. So far, chitosan and salicylate have not been investigated as a combination for the development of multifunctional coatings on Mg alloys. In addition, the self-healing effect of salicylate ions in a chitosan coating was established for the first time.

head.ce:title: Corrosion protection and antibacterial performance of a chitosan/salicylate coating electrogenerated on a magnesium alloy

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this work, chitosan and chitosan/salicylate coatings were prepared by EPD on AZ91D Mg alloy to improve the corrosion resistance of the substrate in a simulated physiological solution. The effect of two concentrations of salicylate on the morphology and anticorrosive behaviour of the coatings was evaluated. The results showed that the addition of 0.50 g/L sodium salicylate to the chitosan formulation decreased the corrosion rate of the chitosan coating from 0.0025 ± 0.0003 to 0.0007 ± 0.0004 mA cm−2. From the results of electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) and Fourier Transform Infrared (FTIR) analysis, it can be concluded that the presence of salicylate in the coating leads to a self-healing effect that contributes to an improved corrosion protection. In addition, the presence of salicylate in the coating has been shown to increase antibacterial activity against both Gram-positive and Gram-negative bacteria. It has been demonstrated that salicylate can be released from the coating, which has an anti-inflammatory effect. In this way, a novel chitosan/salicylate coating was developed for the first time on a Mg alloy for bactericidal effect, release of salicylate and corrosion protection in simulated physiological solution. So far, chitosan and salicylate have not been investigated as a combination for the development of multifunctional coatings on Mg alloys. In addition, the self-healing effect of salicylate ions in a chitosan coating was established for the first time.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Nowadays, Mg and its alloys are the focus of attention for bone implants investigation. They are considered the most promising biodegradable materials for temporary medical applications currently studied (Delavar et al., 2023). Their excellent mechanical properties, biocompatibility, and biodegradability make them a suitable choice for orthopaedic implants and stents (Tong et al., 2022). However, the rapid biodegradation of Mg under physiological conditions hinders the possibility of its use in the manufacture of implants. This excessive degradation negatively affects the mechanical integrity of Mg-based implants, causing them to lose their properties before the tissue is fully healed (Wang et al., 2023). Mg degradation produces Mg2+ and H2. The first is harmless to the human body because the Mg2+ ion is an important mineral for some metabolic activities (Venkatesh and Kanagasabapathy, 2023). However, the H2 gas around the implant device can delay tissue healing and cause haemolysis and inflammation (Tong et al., 2022) (Tian et al., 2023). In order to solve the problem of the rapid degradation of magnesium-based materials, researchers have been studying the possibility of alloying the material (Adithya Garimella and Bandhu Ghosh, 2022), adding reinforcements to develop magnesium matrix biocomposites (Kujur et al., 2021) and modifying their surface (Tong et al., 2022). Surface modification with biocompatible protective coatings is one of the most cost-effective and efficient methods (Tian et al., 2022).

Another major cause of implant failure is bacterial infection, which can lead to serious post clinical complications. Bacterial adhesion and biofilm formation are critical because they are immune to antibiotic treatment. The development of coatings with antibacterial activity has become a viable strategy to handle antibiotic resistance (Kaur et al., 2023).

Natural polymers have attracted attention due to their biocompatibility, biodegradability, and promotion of cell adhesion and proliferation, making them safe and sustainable sources for the development of various biomaterials (Lu et al., 2023). Biopolymers such as phytic acid, gelatin and chitosan have been used to prepare coatings on magnesium alloys for biomedical applications (Zhen et al., 2015). Among biocompatible biopolymers, chitosan is a hydrophilic polysaccharide obtained by deacetylation of chitin and is characterized by its versatility, availability, biodegradability and antimicrobial properties (Khan and Alamry, 2021). The degradation of chitosan produces harmless amino sugars that can be absorbed by human tissues (Khan and Alamry, 2021). In addition, chitosan has excellent film-forming ability and good properties to be employed in controlled drug release applications (da Silva et al., 2020). For all these reasons, chitosan is a promising material for biomedical applications such as the development of biocompatible protective coatings on Mg alloys (Wang et al., 2023) (Kalaiyarasan et al., 2023) with drug delivery properties (da Silva et al., 2020). Even though chitosan has antibacterial properties, the effect can be weak if it is used alone, hence, chitosan is often combined together with other antibacterial components (Kaur et al., 2023) (Hu et al., 2021).

Salicylates (such as aspirin and sodium salicylate) are one of the most popular anti-inflammatory drugs (Amann and Peskar, 2002). They also showed several desirable pharmacological effects such as anticancer, antipyretic and antidiabetic effects (Yeasmin and Choi, 2020). The combination of chitosan with salicylates has been investigated for applications such as drug carriers (Sampedro-Guerrero et al., 2022), plant care and cultivation (Khan et al., 2019) (Novikova et al., 2022) and food safety and preservation (de Menezes et al., 2024). In addition, the antibacterial activity of salicylates has been studied. It has been reported that sodium salicylate in combination with glucose oxidase, can enhance bactericidal activity against Escherichia coli and Staphylococcus aureus (Kaplan, 1973). Salicylate has also been shown to enhance the activity of antibiotics such as gentamicin and cefotaxime (El-Naggar, 1992).

Electrophoretic deposition (EPD) is widely employed as a coating technique. In this method, charged particles in suspension are electrophoretically deposited on an oppositely charged working electrode (Saji, 2021). EPD can be either cathodic or anodic, depending on the nature of the deposition electrode and the charge of the particles. Moreover, EPD is a low-cost technique that can be applied in a simple electrolytic cell setup. Chitosan-based coatings prepared by EPD have been extensively studied for biomedical applications (Avcu et al., 2019). In mildly acidic conditions, such as in an acetic acid solution, the amino groups of chitosan are protonated, allowing it to deposit on the cathode by applying voltage (Saji, 2021) (Avcu et al., 2019).

Improving the corrosion resistance of Mg alloys in physiological environments, as well as their anti-inflammatory and antibacterial properties are key issues for the use of Mg as a degradable implant material (Venkatesh and Kanagasabapathy, 2023) (Tian et al., 2023). The challenge is to develop biocompatible protective coatings with multifunctional behaviour. Incorporation of compounds with bactericidal and anti-inflammatory properties is an effective strategy to accomplish these requirements.

In accordance with the challenge mentioned earlier, the aim of this study is to develop a novel biocompatible protective coating with antibacterial and anti-inflammatory properties for the AZ91D Mg alloy. The AZ91D Mg alloy is commonly used in the study of biomedical applications (Fan et al., 2023) (Li et al., 2023a). As mentioned previously, chitosan/salicylate (CS/Sa) materials have been studied with various applications such as plant care and food safety. However, CS/Sa coatings prepared on Mg alloys for implant materials applications have not been explored yet. In this study, a chitosan-based coating was applied to the AZ91D Mg alloy using the EPD technique. Sodium salicylate (Sa) was introduced into the chitosan formation solution to enhance the anticorrosive and antibacterial properties, as well as to impart an anti-inflammatory function to the substrate. The focus here is on the corrosion studies of the coatings in simulated body fluid solution and the release of an anti-inflammatory drug from the implant material. Additionally, the antibacterial activity of the coatings against bacteria commonly found in implants, such as E. coli and S. aureus, was evaluated. Thus, for the first time, the combination of chitosan and salicylate for the development of a biocompatible composite coating with anti-inflammatory, antibacterial, and anticorrosive properties on a Mg alloy was investigated. The effect of adding different concentrations of Sa to the chitosan solution on the anticorrosive properties of the obtained coatings was assessed. Various surface analysis techniques, including SEM, EDX, X-ray diffraction, and FTIR, were employed to characterize the coatings. The anti-corrosive performance of the films was assessed in Ringer solution at 37 °C through polarization studies, and faradaic impedance spectroscopy (EIS). The findings indicate that adding the appropriate amount of the compound enhances the anticorrosive properties of the chitosan coating. This improvement is attributed to a self-healing effect facilitated by the salicylate ions.

Electrodes of AZ91D Mg alloy with an exposed area of 0.070 cm2 were prepared with a composition of 8.978% Al, 0.6172% Zn, 0.2373% Mn, 0.2987% Si, 0.1189% Cu, 0.00256% Ni, 0.0176% Fe, 0.00164% Ca, 0.01154% Zr, balance Mg. Before each experiment, the electrodes were wet-abraded with grinding paper #1000 and then cleaned repeatedly with distilled water and acetone. For coatings formation, EPD was performed with a two-electrode system, where the pre-treated Mg alloy was used as the working electrode and a 304 stainless steel foil with dimensions of 1 × 1 cm2 as the counter electrode, with an electrode distance of 1 cm. The corrosive behaviour of the samples was evaluated in a traditional three-electrode configuration employing Ag/AgClsat as the reference electrode and a platinum sheet as a counter electrode (Loperena et al., 2016). Electrochemical measurements were carried on using potentiostat/galvanostat AUTOLAB PGSTAT 128 N equipped with a Voltage Multiplier (AUTOLAB) and AUTOLAB PGSTAT 204 equipped with a frequency response analysis module (FRA module).

Chitosan (CS) with 95–96 % deacetylation degree was purchased from Tetrahedron, glacial acetic acid (99.7 %) was purchased from Mallinckrodt and Sodium salicylate (NaSa) purchased from Sigma-Aldrich. All the employed chemicals were analytical grade.

As chitosan needs to be dissolved in acidic medium, during the formation of the film on Mg alloy corrosion will occur. Thus, the pretreatment of the substrate surface was needed (da Silva et al., 2020) (Gao et al., 2019). Before electrophoretic deposition in acidic media, the surfaces of Mg alloy electrodes were treated in 5 M NaOH solution at 60 °C for 2 h. Then, the electrodes were dried at 60 °C during 1 h. The pretreated sample was named as Mg–OH.

Based on the conditions used by other authors for the deposition of chitosan coatings on Mg-based alloys (Avcu et al., 2019), different concentrations of the components of the chitosan solution were evaluated. It was decided to prepare the CS solution by adding 0.15 % (w/w) of chitosan to 15 % (v/v) deionized water, 1 % (v/v) acetic acid and 85 % (v/v) ethanol. It was magnetically stirred for 6 h at 20 ± 5 °C. Ethanol was employed to reduce hydrogen production during EPD (Tian et al., 2023). Afterwards, two different concentrations of sodium salicylate (0.25 and 0.50 g/L) were dispersed in the chitosan solution and stirred for 1 h. Coatings generation was done by electrophoretic deposition (EPD) in solutions containing only chitosan and chitosan with sodium salicylate. Electrodeposition was conducted via the potentiostatic method at 20 ± 5 °C, with an applied potential of 20 V during 20 min. It was determined that the formation of coatings during more than 20 min leads to a very high coating wet weight, which caused it to fall off the electrode. Then, the EPD process was repeated three times in order to obtain a thicker coating. After electrodeposition of each layer, the samples were rinsed with deionized water and dry at room air. Coatings were named as CS for the case of the samples with only chitosan and CS/Sa-25 and CS/Sa-50 for the samples prepared from chitosan solutions containing 0.25 and 0.50 g/L Sa, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Chemical and morphological characterization of the coatings
ce:section.ce:para: To investigate the surface morphology and elemental composition of the CS and CS/Sa samples, a Scanning Electron Microscope (dual stage ISI DS 130 SEM) equipped with energy dispersive spectrometry (EDAX 9600) was employed. The chemical composition of the coatings was analysed by Attenuated total reflectance Fourier transform infrared (ATR-FTIR) spectroscopy using a Nicolet iS5 spectrometer equipped with an iD7 ATR module. ATR-FTIR spectra were recorded in the range of 4000–500 cm−1 at a resolution of 4 cm−1. X-ray diffraction (XRD) analysis were recorded in the range 2θ from 3° to 80° with a Rigaku D-Max III-C diffractometer using Cu Ka radiation and a graphite monochromator.

The corrosion behaviour of the coatings was evaluated in Ringer solution (composition per L: 8.60 g NaCl, 0.30 g KCl and 0.32 g CaCl2.2H2O) at 37 °C by the potentiodynamic method and the electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS). Tafel tests were carried out by polarizing from cathodic to anodic potentials in the potential range of - 0.3 V–0.3 V with reference to the open circuit potential value at 0.001 V s−1. The impedance measurements were performed at the open circuit potential with an amplitude of 10 mV in the frequency range of 105 Hz to 10−2 Hz for the coated samples and to 10−1 Hz for the uncoated substrate. The EIS data were analysed and modelled into equivalent electrical circuits (EEC) with ZView software. All experiments were conducted after the steady state. Each set of experiments was repeated at least three times to ensure repeatability and the data is expressed as the mean ± standard deviation (SD).

The quantity of Mg released in Ringer solution was determined by atomic absorption spectroscopy. The employed equipment was a AA PerkinElmer Analyst 700 spectrophotometer. The covered and uncovered samples were immersed in 50 mL of Ringer solution at 37 °C for different periods of time, then the solutions were homogenized and analysed.

The equipment employed consisted in a beaker containing 250 mL of Ringer solution at 37 °C where the samples with 1 cm2 exposed area were immersed. The volume of the released gas was measured with the use of an inverted funnel and a graduated burette placed over the sample. The solution level was intermittently measured for 8 days and the Ringer solution was replaced by fresh solution every 1-day period. Three independent measurements under each experimental condition were performed to ensure repeatability of the test results.

Sodium salicylate has the maximum UV absorption at a wavelength of 296 nm. The standard calibration curve was obtained in the range of 5 × 10−4 - 2 × 10−6 M sodium salicylate in Ringer solution. The absorbance of the samples was measured using a spectrophotometer UV–vis (SHIMADZU UV-1280). Coated samples were accurately washed to remove any traces of salicylate and then immersed in 50 mL of Ringer solution at 37 °C. At different immersion times, 4 mL of the Ringer solution was taken (1 h, 4 h, 8 h, 24 h, 48 h, 72 h) for UV absorbance measurement, and 4 mL of fresh solution was added to keep constant volume. The samples were in triplicate and the presented values correspond to the average results. Before absorbance measurement, solution was filtered to remove any coating traces that could be released in the medium.
The antibacterial properties of composites were tested against two reference strains, Escherichia coli ATCC 25922 and Staphylococcus aureus ATCC 25923.

The antimicrobial effectiveness of the coatings was tested using the Kirby-Bauer disk diffusion method, following the model of antibiogram execution. Briefly, cultures of the strains under study were cultivated on Mueller–Hinton agar (MHA) plates from Britania Laboratories S.A., Argentina, for 24 h at 37 °C. The resulting colonies were suspended in Mueller–Hinton broth from the same manufacturer. The turbidity (expressed as optical density; OD) of the bacterial suspensions was measured using an optical spectrophotometer (λ = 600 nm). The suspensions were adjusted to a turbidity of 0.5 based on the McFarland standard (106 cell. mL-1). A sterilized cotton swab was dipped into the resulting suspension, and used to apply a bacterial lawn on MHA plates. Petri plates were left to dry for 10 min, after which the composites were distributed. Incubation was carried out at 37 °C, and after 24 h, the plates were examined to identify the presence or absence of zones of inhibition. When zones of inhibition were observed, their diameter was measured with a ruler with a resolution of up to 1 mm.

Fig. 1 displays the micrographs of the different sample surfaces. As mentioned above, the surface of the AZ91D Mg alloy must be pretreated, since the chitosan solution is acidic and the untreated surface of the alloy would corrode easily. After NaOH treatment, a grey Mg(OH)2 film was formed on the surface of the Mg alloy (Pan et al., 2017). The surface of the treated sample (Fig. 1a) exhibits considerable roughness due to the formation of microspheres and microsheets, which is in accordance with the literature (Zhao et al., 2023). The EDX spectrum (Fig. 1b) shows the signals of Mg, O and Na. The CS film (Fig. 1c) is characterized by a smooth surface. At higher magnification, some microspheres can be seen on the surface. According to the literature, the formation mechanism of those particles is not clear, however, it could be associated to the inter/intra-molecular cross-linking of polycationic chitosan by an anionic cross-linker (Mena-Morcillo et al., 2021). Elemental characterization of both the film and microspheres (Fig. 1d) shows the expected qualitative composition of chitosan (C, O and N). Mg and Na signals from the pretreatment are also visible. It is worth to mention that during the studies it was found that a CS film formed onto the untreated AZ91D surface has very poor adhesion to the substrate. This could be related to the rapid dissolution of the Mg based substrate during the EPD process when it was no pretreated, which was evidenced by the large amount of H2 bubble formation on the electrode. In addition, the roughness of the pretreated substrate could promote better adhesion of the CS film to the surface.

To evaluate the effect of sodium salicylate on the surface morphology of CS/Sa coatings, SEM examination was conducted (Fig. 2 ). Fig. 2a and c shows that the addition of Sa does not lead to a significant morphology change compared to the CS coating. The EDX spectra of the coatings show the presence of N from chitosan, C and O from sodium salicylate and chitosan, and the Na signal can be attributed to the pretreatment film and sodium salicylate. For all cases, the peaks at 2.2 and 9.7 keV on the EDX spectra correspond to Au, which was used to metallize the samples.

The thickness of the NaOH pretreatment layer, the SA modified and unmodified CS coatings was measured by examining the cross sectional view of the samples by SEM (Fig. 3 ). Fig. 3a shows that the treatment of the Mg-based substrate in the NaOH solution results in a very thin layer, which cannot be seen in the cross-sectional images of the biopolymer coatings. On the other hand, it is worth mentioning that the hiopolymer coatings appear to be detached from the epoxy material employed for the sample preparation or from the substrate surface. This occurred after polishing the cross section of the samples, likely due to the mechanical force exerted by the polishing machine. The CS coating has a uniform thickness of approximately 32 μm (Fig. 3b). After the addition of SA, the coating thickness increases and becomes less uniform. An average thickness of 42 μm was measured for the CS/Sa-25 sample (Fig. 3c), while the CS/Sa-50 coating had an average thickness of 61 μm (Fig. 3d).

Fig. 4 exhibits the ATR-FTIR spectra of the uncoated AZ91D Mg alloy, the CS coating and a CS/Sa sample. As expected, no signal was detected for the uncoated alloy surface (Gao et al., 2019). The CS coating exhibits a broad band between 3000 and 3500 cm−1 attributed to the overlapped of O–H stretching vibration and N–H stretching of amine and amide groups (Zheludkevich et al., 2011) (Witecka et al., 2021). Peaks at 2920 and 2875 cm−1 are assigned to the C–H vibration of –CH3 and –CH2, respectively (Witecka et al., 2021) (Qi et al., 2023). Small band at 1638 cm−1 is related to stretching vibration of amide I (Wang et al., 2023) (da Silva et al., 2020). Bands at around 1540 and 1400 cm−1 are characteristic of chitosan and are attributed to the amine group (Witecka et al., 2021) (Kalaiyarasan et al., 2023). Near 1340 cm−1, the amide III band of chitosan molecule is represented (Qi et al., 2023). Bands at 1060 and 1020 cm−2 correspond to the C–O vibration of CS, whereas bands at 893 and 1152 cm−1 represent the saccharide structure of CS (Witecka et al., 2021). When the CS coating is modified with salicylate (CS/Sa coating), some bands are affected as a result of the interaction between the components (da Silva et al., 2020). The intensity of the broad band between 3000 and 3500 cm−1 decrease. Moreover, as it was reported by other authors, the band at 1540 cm−1 of the N–H flexion vibration of the amino group decreased and exhibits a shift to the left, indicating that the amine was changed to an amide (Hu et al., 2021). Also, the existence of new bands between 1400 and 1540 cm−1 can be associated with the CC stretching of conjugated salicylic acid (Hu et al., 2021). The observed changes in the FTIR bands when Sa is added to the CS film can be attributed to the existence of an amide bond between the amino group of CS and the carboxyl group of the salicylic acid (Hu et al., 2021).

Chitosan is insoluble in water. However, in acidic solutions the amino group of the biopolymer combines with hydrogen ions to form soluble, protonated chitosan (Ec. 1). The electrophoretic deposition of chitosan coatings was clearly stablished in the literature (Wang et al., 2023) (Oliveira et al., 2021). When the electric field is applied, the positively charged chitosan molecule in the electrolyte is attracted to the negatively charged electrode (the Mg alloy electrode, which acts as the cathode). At the cathode, proton reduction reaction occurs releasing H2. The consumption of hydrogen ions creates a high pH area around the electrode surface, promoting the deprotonation of the chitosan (Ec. 2), which becomes insoluble again and is deposited on the cathode surface, creating the chitosan coating.

When sodium salicylate is added to the aqueous solution, the following reactions can occur. First, the dissociation of acetic acid in aqueous medium to give acetate ions and protons (Ec. 3). Since sodium salicylate is soluble in water and ethanol, dissociation of the salt in aqueous medium leads to salicylate anions and sodium cations (Ec. 4). The combination of the salicylate ion with proton and the acetate ion with protonated sodium gives salicylic acid and sodium acetate, respectively (Ec. 5).

According to Ec. 3 to 5, the electrolyte for the CS/Sa coating formation is a combination of chitosan and salicylic acid. As it was reported, chitosan can be employed for the co-deposition of compounds by EPD technique (Wu et al., 2010) (Al-Rashidy et al., 2018). Salicylic acid molecules in the chitosan solution are surrounded by adsorbed protonated chitosan macromolecules. The salicylic acid with adsorbed chitosan have a net positive charge that can move toward the cathode under the applied electric field in the EPD process (Avcu et al., 2019). Therefore, the chitosan chains encapsulate the salicylic acid molecules to deposit on the surface of the Mg alloy creating a coating composed of chitosan and salicylic acid (CS/Sa samples). Furthermore, the presence of salicylic acid in the CS/Sa coatings was confirmed by the FTIR analysis.

X-ray diffraction analysis was carried out to evaluate the crystallinity of the chitosan coatings. Fig. 5 presents the obtained spectra for the chitosan powder compared with the CS and CS/Sa coatings. As seen from the analysis, the chitosan powder presents two peaks at 2θ values of 10° and 20°, which are representative of the semicrystalline structure of chitosan. However, the spectra of the CS and CS/Sa coated samples do not present the same peaks as the chitosan powder. It has been reported that the EPD technique for chitosan coating formation can decrease the crystallinity of the polymer, especially if the applied potential is high. According to the literature (Oliveira et al., 2021) (Varlık et al., 2022), the absence of signals related to the biopolymer in XRD analysis can be attributed to the breakdown of intermolecular interactions in the chitosan chains (such as the hydrogen bonds between the hydroxyl and amine groups) during the EPD process. The peaks in the CS and CS/Sa spectra are assigned to the Mg based substrate and the Mg–OH layer. These signals can be detected because the thickness of the coatings is not sufficient to prevent X-ray diffraction. As the CS/Sa coating is thicker than the CS coating, the signals appear less intense for the Sa modified sample. The signals for sodium salicylate or salicylic acid in the CS/Sa coating should be visible at 2θ between 10° and 30°. However, the amount of these compounds in the coating could be too low to be detected.

The Tafel polarization curves in Ringer solution for the uncoated and coated Mg alloy are presented in Fig. 6 . The parameters obtained from extrapolation of the curves are summarized in Table 1 . The curve for the untreated AZ91D Mg alloy has been published previously (Loperena et al., 2016), and from its extrapolation, the values for Ecorr and icorr were – 1.501 ± 0.050 V vs Ag/AgClsat and 0.1050 ± 0.0050 mA cm−2, respectively. It is known that icorr is a dynamic parameter directly related to the corrosion rate of the sample. Compared with the uncoated substrate, the corrosion potential of the CS sample is positively shifted and the corrosion current density is lower, indicating that the chitosan coating protects the alloy from corrosion. For the samples modified with Sa, it is noted that the CS/Sa-25 coating has a higher icorr compared to the CS coating (Table 1). On the contrary, the CS/Sa-50 coating has the lowest corrosion current density (icorr = 0.0007 mA cm−2) among the coated samples, indicating higher corrosion resistance. Therefore, the corrosion current values of the coated samples are arranged as follows: CS/Sa-25 > CS > CS/Sa-50. On the other hand, it should be noted that the corrosion potential of the CS/Sa-25 sample is slightly more positive than that of the CS/Sa-50 sample. This can be attributed to the fact that the Ecorr takes into account the tendency for corrosion reactions to occur, as it is a thermodynamic parameter (Hairong Dong et al., 2023). The results show a reduction of icorr by 3 orders of magnitude in the case of the CS/Sa-50 sample compared to the uncoated alloy. This is a very promising result considering that other recent chitosan-based composite coatings applied to Mg alloys have shown a reduction in icorr of 1 or 2 orders of magnitude compared to the uncoated substrate (Tian et al., 2023) (Mena-Morcillo et al., 2021) (Zhang et al., 2023) (Vahedi et al., 2023). Up to this point, the corrosion protection of the coatings can be related to the barrier properties.

To better understand the anticorrosive behaviour of the coatings in the long term, impedance measurements were performed in Ringer solution. Fig. 7 shows the Nyquist and Bode plots for the uncoated alloy and the coated samples after 1 h immersion in the corrosive media. The results were fitted using the equivalent circuits illustrated in Fig. 9, and the parameters obtained are listed in Table 2 . The Nyquist curve of the AZ91D Mg alloy (Fig. 7a) shows one capacitive semicircle and one inductive loop at low frequencies (Arthanari and Shin, 2018). Two different behaviours are observed for the coated samples. The Nyquist plots of CS and CS/Sa-25 (Fig. 7a) show two capacitive loops at the high and medium frequencies and one inductive loop at low frequencies. In contrast, the Nyquist curve of CS/Sa-50 shows two capacitive loops and no inductive loop was observed (Fig. 7a). The semicircle at high frequencies is related to the coating properties, while the semicircle at low frequencies is related to the properties of the double layer (Beyzavi et al., 2023). In general, the diameter of the capacitive loop is related to the corrosion resistance of the sample, while the inductive loop is related to adsorption/desorption process in the sample surface, which corresponds to the corrosion process (Li et al., 2023b). The CS and CS/Sa-50 coated samples show larger capacitive diameters than the uncoated alloy (Fig. 7a), indicating that these coatings protect the substrate from corrosion. The CS/Sa-50 coating has the higher diameter capacitive loop and no inductive loop, indicating that it provides better corrosion protection than the unmodified CS coating. From the impedance Bode plots, the value of |Z| in the low frequency range (1–0.01 Hz) represents the ability of the coating to protect the substrate. The higher the value of |Z|, the better the corrosion resistance (Wang et al., 2023). As it can be seen, the CS/Sa-50 sample has the highest values among the samples (Fig. 7b), indicating better corrosion protection. Moreover, the inductive response of the CS, and CS/Sa-25 specimens can be seen in Bode plots at frequencies below 1 Hz, where a decreasing impedance magnitude (Fig. 7b) and positive phase angles (Fig. 7c) can be observed (King et al., 2014).

For better interpretation, ZView software was used to propose equivalent electrical circuits (Fig. 9). The circuit employed to fit the data of the uncoated AZ91D Mg alloy consists of Rs, Rct and CPEc which are the solution resistance, the charge transfer resistance and capacitive reactance, respectively (Fig. 9a) (Duan et al., 2023). RL and L correspond to the inductive response. For the CS and CS/Sa-25, the equivalent circuit used to fit the results is depicted in Fig. 9b, where RS is the solution resistance, CPEc and Rc are related to the capacitance and resistance of the coating, CPEdl and Rct represent the electronic double layer capacitance and charge transfer resistance, and the inductive behaviour and resistance caused by the degradation are represented by L and RL, respectively. Constant phase element is used instead of capacitor due to the inhomogeneous morphology of the coating (Tian et al., 2023) (Kamde et al., 2022). The sample CS/Sa-50 has to be fitted with a different equivalent circuit (Fig. 9c), which also consist of Rs, Rc, Rct, CPEc and CPEdl, but does not contain inductive elements (Tian et al., 2023). From Tables 2 and it can be seen that all coatings have higher coating resistance (Rc) than the uncoated alloy. For the coated samples, the trend is CS/Sa-50 > CS > CS/Sa-25, indicating that modification with 0.25 g/L Sa does not improve the corrosion resistance of the CS coating. The addition of 0.50 g/L Sa leads to the formation of a coating with better anticorrosive properties. This is consistent with the Tafel polarization results. Moreover, it was mentioned earlier that the low-frequency capacitive loop of the impedance responses of the coated samples is related to the properties of the bilayer (Kamde et al., 2022). Thus, the Rct represents the corrosion tendency at the interface between the coating and the substrate due to the penetration of corrosive ions (Liu et al., 2023). The higher value of Rct for the CS/Sa-50 specimen compared to the other coated samples indicates that this coating provides better protection for the alloy surface.

It is worth noting that no increase in total impedance with immersion time was observed in the CS coating (Supplementary Material). The increase in impedance with immersion time for the CS/Sa-50 sample is therefore related to the self-healing properties of the coating that contains salicylate (Calado et al., 2020) (Dai et al., 2023). Salicylate is known to act as a passivating agent for Mg-based materials (Ascencio et al., 2018). The combination of salicylate anion present in the electrolyte with Mg cations originated from Mg dissolution gives magnesium salicylate (Francis et al., 2015) (Grubač et al., 2016).

In the initial phase of immersion, the coatings act as a physical barrier that prevents the aggressive ions from coming into contact with the substrate surface. Fig. 7 shows that the CS and CS/Sa-50 coatings provide similarly good corrosion protection after 1 h of immersion. The CS/Sa-25 coating exhibits lower corrosion protection, suggesting that the addition of a lower concentration of sodium salicylate in the chitosan solution has a negative effect on the anticorrosive properties. With increasing immersion time, the barrier effect of the CS coating becomes weaker, so that the corrosive media come into contact with the substrate and lose their corrosion protective properties. In the case of the CS/Sa-50 coating, as the immersion time increases and the aggressive ions penetrate the coating, it releases salicylate anions and magnesium cations (Fig. 10 ). The combination of the two ions released leads to the formation of magnesium salicylate (Mg(Sa)). This compound is deposited on the substrate surface and forms a passivating film that provides corrosion protection. Thus, when the sample coated with CS/Sa-50 is exposed to a Ringer solution, an improvement in the corrosion properties can be noticed. This behaviour can be observed for up to 7 days. After that, the coating is further degraded by the corrosive medium until it loses its anticorrosive properties. In summary, the impedance results show that the CS/Sa-50 coating not only provides a barrier effect between the aggressive media and the substrate surface, but also has self-healing properties that improve corrosion protection performance.

To better understand the anticorrosive and self-healing properties of the CS/Sa-50 coating, Fig. 11 shows the FTIR spectrum and SEM images of the sample after immersion in Ringer solution. Compared to the CS/Sa-50 sample without immersion (Fig. 4), two new bands at 1365 and 1475 cm−1 appear in the FTIR spectrum after immersion of the sample in Ringer solution. According to the literature, the peaks are related to the formation of asymmetric and symmetric carboxylate peaks, indicating the formation of the magnesium salicylate complex (Fockaert et al., 2020). The self-healing behaviour of the CS/Sa-50 coating proposed above is verified by the FTIR analysis and is related to the formation of magnesium salicylate, which is deposited on the sample surface and causes the improvement in the anticorrosive properties observed in the impedance measurements.

From the SEM images (Fig. 11b), it can be seen that the surface of the coating shows changes compared to the surface before immersion (Fig. 2c). The smooth morphology is not seen and some microstructures appeared on the surface. However, there is no evidence of several corrosion. Therefore, the coating continues to act as a physical barrier between the substrate and the corrosive media and protects the magnesium surface.

In order to verify the improvement of the anticorrosive properties of the CS coating after modification with Sa, the release of Mg in Ringer solution was measured for the CS and CS/Sa-50 coated samples after different exposure times. The obtained values are presented in Table 3 . As can be seen from the results, after 5 h of immersion, the CS and CS/Sa-50 coated samples retard Mg dissolution by 58 and 69 %, respectively. After 5 days of immersion, the CS/Sa-50 coated sample retards Mg dissolution by about 25% compared to the CS sample, proving that the addition of Sa enhances the protective properties of the chitosan coating.

When the Mg alloy is immersed in the chloride containing solution, it dissolves immediately, producing H2 and Mg(OH)2 as corrosion products (Ec. 6). The Mg(OH)2 forms a weak protective layer on the Mg substrate surface, which dissolves rapidly in Ringer solution (Dong et al., 2022). Then, Cl− easily penetrates the surface film and the substrate starts to corrode. Since the formation of corrosion products does not affect H2 evolution, the evolution of the released gas volume is a valid method to evaluate substrate degradation (Chun-Yan et al., 2010).

Fig. 12 presents the cumulative H2 volume evolution in Ringer solution over time for the uncoated AZ91D Mg alloy and the coating that exhibited the best anticorrosive behaviour, CS/Sa-50. It is clear from the curves that the CS/Sa-50 coating reduces the hydrogen evolution rate of the substrate. The coating acts as a physical barrier preventing the contact between the substrate and the corrosive solution and providing corrosion protection. As mentioned earlier, the Sa− released from the coating combines with Mg2+ to form magnesium salicylate, which continues to protect the substrate. However, as the immersion time increases, the coating becomes weaker and the corrosive ions penetrate it, getting in contact with the substrate surface. Thus, from the curves it can be seen that the amount of H2 volume released after 8 days for the CS/Sa-50 sample is the same as the amount released after 3 days for the uncoated substrate.

As it was mentioned before, inflammation post-surgery is a critical issue. Salicylate is one of the most commonly used anti-inflammatory drugs. To evaluate the release of salicylate from the CS/Sa-50 coating over time, the concentration of the compound in Ringer solution was calculated by measuring the absorbance in a UV spectrophotometer at a wavelength of 296 nm. As can be seen from the curve (Fig. 13 ), Sa is released faster in the first 8 h. In the following 64 h, the Sa concentration remains almost unchanged. This behaviour could be advantageous because a faster release of the drug may have an anti-inflammatory effect during the critical early phase of implantation (Tian et al., 2023). Thereafter, the coating continues to release Sa, which is important for maintaining the effect.

The antibacterial activity of the CS and CS/Sa-50 coated samples was evaluated by the inhibition zone method against E. coli (Gram positive) and S. aureus (Gram negative) (Fig. 14 ). It is known that chitosan has antibacterial activity (Raspo et al., 2022). In this case, the CS coating shows an inhibition zone diameter of 9.0 ± 1.0 mm for E. coli and 6 ± 1.0 mm for S. aureus. The CS/Sa-50 coating shows an inhibition zone diameter of 14.5 ± 1.0 mm for E. coli and 11.0 ± 1.0 mm for S. aureus. These results confirm that the addition of sodium salicylate to the chitosan formation solution leads to the formation of CS/Sa coatings with increased antibacterial activity.

In the present work, we successfully developed a novel multifunctional CS/Sa-50 coating using EPD method on AZ91D Mg alloy. The good anticorrosive performance of the CS/Sa-50 coating can be attributed to both its effective physical barrier properties and the formation of magnesium salicylate, which led to the self-healing behaviour. Moreover, our study revealed that the CS/Sa-50 coating enhances the antibacterial properties of the CS coating against both E. coli and S. aureus. In addition, the release of anti-inflammatory salicylate from the CS/Sa-50 coating in simulated physiological media further improves its potential in biomedical applications.

This research marks for the first time the use of the combined properties of chitosan and salicylate to create a coating for Mg alloy, addressing the critical requirements for implant materials, including corrosion resistance, biocompatibility, antibacterial and anti-inflammatory drug delivery. The integration of these elements represents a significant advancement in the search of suitable Mg-based materials for implant applications. To advance our understanding and contribute to the field of biomedical materials, our next phase of research involves subjecting the coating to in vivo tests.
